{{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m
Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Burial Mounds 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest burial mound in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Burial Mound Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * [https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/012/index.html Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi] – Imperial Household Agency * {{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m
Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Burial Mounds 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest burial mound in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Burial Mound Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * [https://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/012/index.html Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi] – Imperial Household Agency * Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Burial Mounds 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest burial mound in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Burial Mound Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m

Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External {{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m
Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Burial Mounds 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest burial mound in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Burial Mound Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m

Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External {{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m
Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Burial Mounds 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest burial mound in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Burial Mound Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Largest burial mound from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest burial mound in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The burial mound is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite burial mounds have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped burial mound to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square burial mound measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m

Largest Kofun from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest Kofun in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The Kofun is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite Kofuns have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * {{Infobox ancient site |name = Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun |image =[[File:Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun, zenkei.jpg|280px]]
Full view of the mound (circular rear on the right, rectangular front on the left). |affiliation = {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}} |location = {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Shibutani Town, Nara Prefecture{{Sfn|Keikō Emperor Mausoleum Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}. |latitude_deg = 34|latitude_min = 33|latitude_sec = 3.00 |longitude_deg = 135|longitude_min = 50|longitude_sec = 56.28 |iso = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size = Length: 300 m
Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Kofuns 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest Kofun in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Kofun Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest Kofun from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest Kofun in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The Kofun is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite Kofuns have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest Kofun in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The Kofun is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite Kofuns have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai Height: 25 m (rear circular part) |construction = Mid-to-late 4th century (circa 321–340 AD[[Amano Sueki]] "Considering the Tombs and Burials of the Imperial Mausoleum" in *The Forefront of Ancient History Research: Imperial Tombs*, Yōsensha, 2016, pp. 61-62.[https://www.city.fujiidera.lg.jp/soshiki/kyoikuiinkai/bunkazaihogo/koramukodaikaranomemessezi/wanogoozidai/10289.html Construction Dates of Giant Kofuns 1 (No. 162)] – Fujidera City Website). |burial_facility = Unknown |burial_person = Designated as Emperor Keikō by the Imperial Household Agency |artifacts = {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, (reported) stone pillow |mausoleum = Designated "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" by the Imperial Household Agency |notes = 8th largest Kofun in Japan / 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ranking of Kofun Sizes in Japan] – Sakai City Website, updated May 13, 2018.
Largest Kofun from the early Kofun period |map = Japan Nara |label = Shibutani Mukoyama
Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest Kofun in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The Kofun is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite Kofuns have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == * Kofun |label_position = top |icon = Keyhole-shaped mound-west }} '''Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun''' (渋谷向山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. It is part of the {{ill|Yanagimoto Kofun Group|ja|柳本古墳群}}. While the actual burial figure is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency designates it as the "Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi" (山邊道上陵), the tomb of Emperor Keikō. This is the 8th largest Kofun in Japan and the 2nd largest in Nara Prefecture, dating back to the mid-to-late 4th century. Recent studies suggest a slightly earlier construction date in the mid-4th century. == Overview == The Kofun is located along the eastern edge of the Nara Basin, on a slope extending westward from Mount Ryuo. The {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|ja|山辺の道}} trail passes near the rear circular portion of the mound, and remnants of ancient stone-paved paths lead toward {{ill|Andon-yama Kofun|ja|行燈山古墳}}. It is managed by the Imperial Household Agency, and multiple investigations have been conducted by the agency’s Shoryobu Division since 1971, as well as academic studies in 2016. The keyhole-shaped mound faces westward (downhill). The rear circular section comprises four tiers, and the rectangular front comprises three tiers. Measuring 300 meters in length, the Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun is the largest in the Yanagimoto Kofun Group and the second-largest in Nara Prefecture after the {{ill|Maruyama Kofun (Kashihara)|ja|見瀬丸山古墳|lt=Maruyama Kofun}} in Kashihara (310 meters). A moat surrounds the mound, and several satellite Kofuns have been identified nearby. Artifacts recovered include {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|8=ja|9=円筒埴輪|lt=cylindrical}} and {{ill|figural Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|lt=figural}} {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a stone pillow designated as an Important Cultural Property. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}: * Total length: 300 meters * Rear circular section: ** Diameter: 168 meters ** Height: 25 meters ** 4-tier construction * Rectangular front section: ** Width: 170 meters ** Height: 23 meters ** 3-tier construction A moat surrounds the mound, segmented into compartments by earthen bridges. These bridges are believed to have been designed to retain water on sloped terrain. While some sections were modified for agricultural use in modern times, others remain in their original state{{R|Shiraishi}}. == Artifacts == [[File:奈良県天理市渋谷出土 石枕.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Stone pillow (Important Cultural Property) traditionally attributed to Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun}}{{small|On display at the Kansai University Museum.}}]] Artifacts recovered during investigations include cylindrical and figurative {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}}, as well as a notable stone pillow attributed to this mound. The stone pillow, unearthed in 1864, is made of jasper, weighs 24 kilograms, and features incised patterns along its edges. It was designated as an Important Cultural Property in 1961 and is currently housed at the Kansai University Museum in Osaka Prefecture{{Sfn|Yamanobe-no-Michi-no-E-no-Misasagi (Heibonsha)|2006}}{{National Important Cultural Property Database|201|9838|Stone Pillow}}[http://www.kansai-u.ac.jp/Museum/depot/detail.php?i=7 Kansai University Museum – Stone Pillow]. Another item traditionally linked to this mound is a triangular-rimmed mirror with patterns, now preserved at the Kyoto National Museum. == Associated Tombs == The Imperial Household Agency recognizes three satellite tombs (designated "I", "Ro", and "Ha"): * Tomb I ("Uenoyama Kofun"): A 144-meter keyhole-shaped Kofun to the north, potentially preceding Shibutani Mukoyama Kofun. * Tomb Ro ("Maruyama Kofun"): A circular mound with a diameter of approximately 35 meters. * Tomb Ha ("Akasaka Kofun"): A square Kofun measuring approximately 35 meters per side. == Related Facilities == * {{ill|Kansai University Museum|ja|関西大学博物館}} (Suita, Osaka) – Displays the stone pillow (Important Cultural Property). == See Also == * {{ill|Emperor Keikō|ja|景行天皇}} == References == == External Links == *
